All right, so your mind can be your biggest ally or your biggest enemy as your writing copy. And I've gone through some periods in my life that were difficult emotionally and we're going to talk about a little bit about that, how to get through those times, and how to get your head right to write the best copy you can write. We talked this morning about this idea, I'm not going to be laborate. Money is life. What you're doing when you write copy, all you're doing, or taking any job, is you're selling a chunk of your life in return for money. And all money then represents is life. It's a proxy for your life. So if money is evil, your life is evil. That's all there is to it. Money is life. So as copywriters, there are, I count six, you may have others, that are really big threats to your success. The first is lack of intensity. The failure to go the extra mile, to make sure that every word is right. Every argument is proven. The failure to invest the skull sweat required to present something that's ultimately very credible and persuasive to your client. Intimidation. All of you in this room, at the point of your careers that you're at, are going to be dealing with intimidation. I have a major problem. Personally, it's a reverse problem. When a copywriter works for me, they're terrified that they'll say something or do something wrong. So rather than giving me a great suggestion that might really put the package over the top, they keep their mouth shut. And the hardest thing in the world is for me, I can tell them a million times, if you don't like my crit on your copy, I want to hear about it and I want to hear why. You can tell them a million times, they won't do it. Copywriters who are getting started have the opposite problem. Lack of confidence in your opinion, in your gut instinct, lack of confidence in your entire situation compared to the people you're working with. You're working with a guy who generates tens or hundreds of millions of dollars a year for a company as a marketing director. And you're a copywriter on your first or second or third or fifth or tenth assignment. That can be very intimidating. And you need to find a way to win through that intimidation. I've got a dear friend and very good copywriter, Brad Peterson, who is the world's worst procrastinator. And I keep trying to tell him, what you do is when the package arrives, you read the research package or the care package that they send you to begin a project, read everything. Drop everything, read everything. If you want to procrastinate, then you procrastinate. But it's in here. And your brain will be chewing on it for a while. And then when you're ready to return to it, when you get to that point where you're anxious, you have some good ideas and you really want to run with it, go with it. I don't know if he's overcome that problem or not. I haven't worked with him in about six months. But when you procrastinate, what you're doing is stealing from yourself. Because you're compressing the amount of time you're going to have to actually get this job done. And that means you'll have less time to work out creative solutions in the copy. Negativity or cynicism. Again, read Dead Puppies for Sale. It's basically about a guy that wrote me a letter that said, I guess I won't be a very good copy. I've been a copywriter for 40 years or something. But I guess I'll never make it to the top. Because I know that these products just, they don't work. Or they don't work for very many people. And so here he was, negative on the very stuff he was trying to sell. And so I made the point that if you were trying to sell a puppy, would you write about the fact that it was really cute, it was adorable, and it would be a faithful friend, and it would be fun to play with, and a big part of your life? Or would you write about the fact that ultimately it's going to die? So you're buying a future tragedy. Both situations are absolutely true, right? Now do you feel compelled when you're selling that puppy? And let's say you're writing about all the wonderful things a puppy will be for you. Do you feel compelled to disclose that it's going to croak? So no, you don't. But this guy only focused on the negative, and it absolutely killed his girl. I told him, you know what, you should probably quit this and go write obituaries for a living. So lack of creativity. Now all creativity is putting two ideas together in a novel or new way. In a way that hasn't happened before. It's a big world, there's nothing new under the sun. But the idea is in your area, in your niche, you're putting two ideas together in a very unique way. If you think of creativity in any other way, then it becomes this diffuse kind of idea that will get you nowhere. But instead if you look at it, you look at a situation or a problem and you say, all right, well what is this like? Well, it's like that. Okay, well what other things have been put together with that? And so you work through the creative process. And finally, depression. Depression is a big one. And I went through a situation where I built a company up from 30 million to 120 million, 125 million dollars in revenues over a fairly short period of time. It was then sold to people who didn't know anything about that business. And I watched my baby basically get killed. I mean, the first thing they did was say that the previous owner of the company whose name was on every email could no longer be used. Even though he was still there in an official capacity in the company. The next thing, it was a coin company. And the next thing they did was they told everybody that we would no longer pay them fair market value for their coins when they went to sell them. We would only pay them on the bullion value of those coins. Well, rare coins are sold. It might have $5 worth of silver in it, but it's worth $500 because of its rarity, right? So when they did that, it killed the entire file. The file said, we don't even want to hear from you anymore. You've screwed us royally. So I was incredibly depressed. And I went to see Dr. Samuelson. And we'll talk a little bit about what I learned from him and how to deal with depression. So how are you going to deal with those things? First of all, intensify. You need to study the masters every year. You hear this a million times and you never do it. This is Ogilvy on advertising. This is a great, great book. Ogilvy was one of the first advertising men to understand that only direct response copywriters really knew how things worked. He had the Rolls Royce account for years, years and years. His partner, Drayton Bird, has driven the rolls every day of his life. And Drayton is the person who taught David about direct mail. It's a direct response. It's an awesome book. Another one, How to Write Good Advertisement by Vic Schwab. These are all on Amazon. Very easy to order. Excellent book. Gene Schwartz's book. I don't have it up here. Also awesome. Wendy has it, I think. I just have the test of advertising. OK. Well, also Gene Schwartz's book. Oh, I'm sorry. Yeah. Yeah, that one. And it's available from Boardroom, but I bought it online. You know, it'll take you a week or two to move through these books, and you'll come away with a mastery of direct response copywriting that a few of your competitors will possess. I've given you a reading list here. And I'd strongly recommend it. I would say it. I'd be so strong as to say it this way. Until you read these books and can quote from them and really get what they say, you're a hazard to your clients. You get that? That's how important they are. These are the guys who created our industry. Intensify. Create Capel's checklist. He likes checklists. It's very easy to adapt his material to checklists. Put successful promotions under the microscope. Somebody was talking about Paris Reverse Engineering, something I did. I learned by reverse engineering everything Gary Benzavenga did. Gary's dad, this is unfair. He got it in the genes. His dad wrote Snap Crackle Pop. He used to always enter these advertising slogan sweepstakes. So when Rice Krispies had one, he wrote Snap Crackle Pop. And that's where he came from. That's where Gary came from. So when he was working for Phillips, he was working for Rosenthal. I was getting this incredible material in the mail. Instead of writing what he wrote, I would say, what was his strategy? What was his outline? How did he put this together? Why did he put things in this order? Why does that make sense? And I loved it. Every time I went to the mailbox, it was like getting a lesson in copywriting. Practice persuasion. Now, I think I'm a natural copywriter. And I wrote an article once that said, everything you need to know about copywriting, you learn in kindergarten. And it was basically that we know as children instinctively how to persuade our parents to do things. At first, our tools are very rudimentary, like screaming your lungs out, like Carlene. But then as we grow older, we start to get a little smarter about that. And at some point, you realize that the way to get what you want from your parents is to give them what they want on something. I wanted a new bicycle, not a new bicycle. I wanted my first bicycle. And so I went to my mom and dad. I said, I really want a bicycle. Well, we don't have the money for that. And I said to my dad, I said, look, I said, you have to drive me to school every day. That takes gasoline, right? Plus, it takes time out of your busy day. You buy me a bike, I'll ride to school. Somehow I knew that instinctively, that giving my dad what he wanted, what he needed, would get me what I wanted. So when you're talking to your spouse and you want a new motorcycle, or you want a new sports car, or ladies, what would it be? A piece of jewelry, whatever it is you want. What? Practice persuasion. Don't be afraid to take them on. Mentally go through the points that you need to make in order to get your spouse to agree to whatever it is you want them to agree with. Do it with your friends, too. Debating is fun. It's healthy. And Wendy and I are political nuts. We're always reading, or watching, or listening to some political message. And the debate in politics is really, it's getting a lot nastier these days. But still, logic wins the day, in most cases. When it doesn't, that's interesting, too, because it'll be a motion that carries the day. And you'll learn how to use emotion in ways, in rhetoric, in order to be persuasive. Practice excellence. There's a whole mindset that goes along with not settling for what is done, but demanding something better. And demanding something better from yourself. It takes, and that is intensity, that is intensity. It comes down to, look, I've written this copy. I think it's fantastic, but it can't be this easy. I need to go through it one more time. We're not talking first draft, like we were talking earlier. On your final, final draft, you don't want to let go of that until you are personally satisfied that you have done everything in your power to write a persuasive piece. Critical thinking. I think this, now, we just talked about how important it is to be positive, right? You don't have a Martin Weiss. I've got a Martin Weiss. Martin Weiss is Mr. Negative. If you give him the best thing in the world, he will overthink it until he finds out something really bad about it. And quite often, his logic is terribly flawed, but by Golly, he fulfilled his role of being Mr. Negative. It forces me to reconsider the points that he brings up. He may say, well, this product is wrong for our follow-up because our people are crisis investors, and this is a stock market product. So now I have to think that through, and I have to decide, well, did I fully value that fact, as I was writing the copy? What objections are my crisis investors going to have to buying stocks today? And it really is good to have that voice. If you don't have it, you have to learn how to develop it yourself so that you have at least one pass of editing on your copy where you look at it and you go, I'm going to find every flaw in this thing. I'm going to find every logic loop, every piece of false logic, every problem I can possibly identify, and I'm going to address it. And then view the world as a marketer. This is just a very general statement. But the idea is you're watching TV at night, and the ads come on. You ask yourselves, what was the agency really trying to accomplish here? Nine times out of 10, you'll see the ad, and you'll go, what were they smoking? When you look at the ad, you say, I wrote an article. What was it? I think it was Frogs. It's the name of the article. But it was about how this agency comes to the head of Budweiser and says, you're really screwing up with your advertising and goes, well, why? And the agency says, no frogs. You know, I mean, frogs in your advertising. So the head of the agency says, frogs, I got to have frogs. The agency says, absolutely. So they do this whole series of commercials about this Budweiser frog sitting on a lily pad and going, bud, bud, like that. So how much is the campaign? Well, it's a $20 million campaign. He goes, well, I need frogs. Got to get frogs. So he goes ahead and he signs off and he gets his frogs. So the campaign is out there, and everybody's talking about these great commercials about frogs and Budweiser. But nobody's buying more beer. The campaign doesn't make them thirsty. It doesn't give them a reason why they should buy bud instead of Miller. Their sales aren't going up. And see, that is the difference between what we do. You can't be a hero in this business without response. So when I was looking at those spots, I was going, what in the world are these guys thinking? What are they smoking? But see, if you're in a business where there's no accountability, you can get away with that stuff. The agency that came up with that got all kinds of awards. Now they're an award-winning agency and so forth. They didn't sell a single can of Budweiser. They can't prove that they did, certainly. So I learned lessons from doing that, from watching that kind of stuff. And I try to take lessons from everything I'm doing. Watching politicians, how they work, they are their consummate marketers. It's all it is. It's one big scam. They're just trying to sell us stuff, trying to sell us a sys still and shut up while I raise our taxes while they control our health care. While they collect money from us that they can give to constituents to buy more votes, that's how it works. That's the scam. And the people like me who get all uptight about politics, I'm buying into that scam. And I know it's happening to me. And I'm still letting it happen. But anyway, you view the world as a marketer. Everything in life is a transaction. So look at those transactions and ask yourself, what's really going on here? Learn from the best online. I'm so thrilled that I was able to know Gary Halbert before he died. His stuff is still just awesome online. And what is it, Bond? His son? Is keeping the legend alive. And the material is up there on the internet. And reading his stuff will blow your mind. Ben Savanga's bullets, Parastil's me. He's not publishing much anymore. But the archives are there. And they're really awesome. Makepiece Total Package, my humble contribution to this. We have probably 150, 200 articles on marketing there. They're all free. There's nothing to buy. Ben Settle, John Carlton, John Ford, and Frank Kern are other favorites of mine. The education you can get just by going to these people's sites, Google them, go to their sites, and read their free stuff is just really mind-boggling. OK, beating intimidation. What do you do? How do you do that? I'm insecure. I'm young. I've just started doing this. How do you deal with the intimidation? Keep several things in mind. First of all, what you do is the difference between success and failure for the companies you work for. They can invent millions of products. They can build lists of millions of leads. They can do every other function that that company does and still fail if they don't have good copy. So you're the scarce resource. So when you come in to a company, keeping that in mind will help. But also keep your eyes on the prize. When I submitted copy to Marty Elstein over at boardroom, he'd send me a balloon bouquet. I think the first time he sent me flowers, I thought that was a little bit. And so he sent me balloon bouquets. They will make you a good client will make you feel like a king or queen. When I went to visit Phillips, they'd have a sign in the lobby, welcome Clayton Makepeace. It would always be a big deal. So that's where you're going to go. Starting out feeling intimidated is fine. Face the fear. Do it anyway. Understand they need you more than you need them. Remember that you're bringing something unique to this. Now, I'm not talking here just about what we mentioned several times, which is having other skills beyond copywriting. I'm talking about you at your spiritual level, who you are. Because nobody else is going to look at that product the way you do. Nobody else is going to have the same promotional ideas you have. What you do is unique to you. It's the output of who you are. So keep that in mind. Rejection isn't fatal unless you let it be. Karleen was the best at this. She feels every emotion like 10 times more than I do. But when she had a bomb and called me up and is yelling, I suck, that's fine. You have to feel the disappointment, the frustration, but then let it go and get on with it. And that's in terms of a promotion. If you're out there and you're putting yourself on the line and everything you stand for and your best ideas and you don't get the job, if you feel like grieving, go ahead and grieve. Take a day. Take a few hours. But you got to get over it. You got to keep going. What we do is all about persistence. Every one of us had situations and points in our careers where it would have been easier to quit than to go on. When you quit, basically, you have to become your worst enemy. And then feel the fear or feel the intimidation and acknowledge it and say, it's OK to feel this way right now. And I'm just going to do it. And then as soon as it's over with, I'm going to move on. Your worst enemy thinks all these things about you. You have little or no value. It doesn't believe you could ever do this. It tells you you don't deserve this. Expects you to fail. Uses setbacks to confirm all of the above. He's telling you this stuff all the time. But the minute you have a setback, he's saying, I told you so. You're no good. You shouldn't have even started this. It tells you you should just quit. Just give it up. Try not to be your own worst enemy. Try not to be the person who's saying these things to you. Procrastination. This is kind of hard because we all have real lives. And things going on in our real lives could be like tension, pressures, frustration, stress, whatever. So it's real easy at certain points in the process to say, nobody will care if I take today off. And nobody will even know. And I'll just go deal with these other issues and so forth. And that's fine to a degree. And it can get excessive, but it's OK to be there. But keep in mind that when you start a project, what you've done is take the first step in a 1,000 mile journey. And don't be intimidated by the amount of work that remains. There's this thing that we do online all the time. It's called the bread crumb sign up. And what it basically is, let's say you're going to buy something from me in the order forms online. And so I need a lot of information from you. I need your name, your street address, your city, state, zip. I need your phone number if there's credit card involved or if I'm shipping FedEx. And I need your credit card number and expiration date and the little number on the back. So I need all that stuff. Well, if I get you to my order form and you look at this daunting amount of information, you're going to abandon the order form. Huge numbers of people do. But if instead your order form says, great, we're glad you ordered, what's your first name? And you fill that in and then another field opens up. Well, what's your last name? Another field opens up. And so they're taking one step at a time with you. Well, that order form idea is extremely powerful. But the same thing is true with every project you take on. You don't have to write the whole damn thing today. All you have to do is read the material. And you have that mindset every day when you get up, instead of looking at the long haul and how much work there is to do, just look at the one thing you have to do today. And it's hard for me. I get up so early in the morning. A lot of times I'm a little groggy and stuff. And I'll just make a bargain with myself. Clay, all you really have to do today is this one little thing. And I'll do it and it'll take like five minutes. And the next thing you know, I'm just typing like crazy. And I've hit my stride for the day. Instead of viewing it as work, a lot of what we do is creative. So when you're doing the right brain stuff, play. Nobody's going to see your copy until you deliver it. You can put anything in there you want. If something makes you smile, makes you laugh, I'm famous for my rough drafts. I'll have a big thing this tall on the front page is big effing headline here. And that's just for me. That makes me smile. I don't know why, but it does. One time a client did see that and thought it was hilarious. But, huh? That's how things like that actually get printed. It was long enough to see that kind of humor actually make it into print. I'm pretty sure with that. People are not happy and not laugh. Yeah. Well, maybe we should make it. One of the points of this is don't send anything out until you've proofread it, right? Proofreading. One time I was doing a proposal for a client and a potential client. And I was listing all of the things we were going to charge him for. And one of them was proofreading. Proofreading. It said pre-fruiting. And it went out that way. OK, discover the art of creative procrastination. We talked about that, how you use your spare time to focus on things that are challenges or wherever you are in the copy at the time. We talked about working like you only have half a brain, doing the right brain stuff at one point of the day, the left brain stuff at another time. And listening to your inner adult. Again, knowing when the inner adult has value to bring to the work you're doing right now. And when you need to tell them to shut up and listen to the child. Synicism. We've talked about this. We've covered it pretty well, I think. You've got to read dead pie. It's funny. So it'll be fun to read it if you can get it. This probably won't work. If you get yourself believing that, you might as well give up the job. Refund the money, quit. Because you're not going to have a success. You need to determine that you believe in the product before you accept the assignment. Use your voice to make every promotion better. We've talked about that. And when you truly can't believe, again, walk away. Walk away. Carline called me one time and she had been offered a really lucrative assignment with somebody in Phoenix, I think, if I remember right. And the promotion was about this ingredient in a supplement. You remember this? Ingredient in a supplement. And there were all kinds of wonderful evidence that this ingredient actually treated this health condition, actually did this thing. And so looks good. Looks like something I can get behind, something I can conscientiously promote, feel good about. But then Carline figured out that the effective dose of this ingredient was here. And the amount you were actually getting in the supplement was way down here. And so there's no way this supplement was going to do. She was going to have to say it did in her promotion. And so she walked away. She just resigned the account. So maybe not the account. You just walked away from the project. When I brought it to the attention of the client, that, hey, it was hyaluronic acid. It was the ingredient. And I said, looks gray, but you don't have what you can promote honestly as being an ingredient. And so I said, I'm not going to write this because it's not true. And he threatened to sue me because he was a former lawyer. And I said, fine, go for it. But that's done. Then he asked for his deposit back. I said, sure, give me my time back. Fair deal. I'm like, I'm helping to keep you out of jail, to keep you from getting in trouble. And I spent over a week doing the research on this. And now we're saying this is a done deal. And he's saying, I want his deposit back. And I'm like, OK, so now I've helped you, but I still lost my time. So he threatened to sue me, everything else. I'm like, you're a jerk. I'm not dealing with you anymore. But here's a moral story about the whole don't burn bridges. And remember, this is a very small, incestuous community that we have. About five or six years later, I get a call from a guy who says, hey, I want you to write for me. I said, OK, sure, no problem. And then I signed up for him. And then when it was time to write the package, he says, oh, actually, you'll be working with this guy. Which was, I said, oh, heck no. There is no way I'm working with a guy. I said, he's a jerk. Anyone who threatens to sue me is not going to get a second chance to work with me. So I have never worked with this guy. And every time his name comes up, and it's come up several times throughout the years, I say the same thing. He's a jerk. I'm not working with him. I won't tell you his name. So he doesn't sue me. Integrity is important. A lot of people hear about copywriters. And they just assume that we'll say whatever it takes to make a sale. We'll make up stories. We'll lie. We'll disemble. We'll mislead. And you need to be better than that. You don't need to stoop to those things. And I had a really close brush with 20 years in prison. I found out that one of my clients was overpricing his product, an investment product. And then telling people they had this huge profit potential. And when I found that out, I resigned the account immediately. And I wrote this great resignation letter. My mama would kill me if she knew I was doing something like this because you're hurting people. And so I resigned the account. Within 60 days, the Federal Trade Commission was in that guy's office. They seized every computer, every file, including my resignation letter. They had him up on RICO charges, a racketeering charges. And with a jail sentence for every single time, he misled a single in one of the people on this file. And we had like 120,000 people on the file. We were mailing him like six times a week. So it was like, if he loses this thing, he's going away forever. Well, he went away for 20 years. But when the Federal Trade Commission saw my resignation letter, they didn't even so much as call me to testify. And I wrote every one of those promotions. So at that point, I was really glad my mama taught me not to lie, cheater, steal. And the more copy you write, the more times you're going to find yourself in situations where you have to decide between right and wrong. Johnny Johnson once told me, he said, always recognize that doing the right thing is the most difficult thing to do and then do it anyway. That's really good advice. Creativity, metaphors and similes, what is this like? When you're looking at a benefit that a product provides, you say, well, what is this like? And you connect it to something else. Remember, it cleans like a white tornado, right? Think about those kinds of things. What is this like? What it does is like what? And that will suggest other things that you can connect to the product or to your pitch that will really get you into that creative groove that you're looking for. The solo creativity, remember, is connectivity, connecting two ideas that were previously not corrected. And waste time willingly exploring every viable path. Now, my negative client will try to kill every idea. The minute he hears it, right? And don't go down that path. Don't do that. No, that's never going to work. But I did this diagram to try to convince him that you can't do that. You've got to go down this path and you'll hit dead end, dead end, dead end, dead end. But if you don't go down those paths, you'll never get to the brilliant. So it's OK as you're writing copy to say, I wonder if this would work. And you just start going down that path a little bit. And maybe you'll identify something there that doesn't work or the logic doesn't work or whatever. And so you say, OK, let's try something else. Try it this way. And if you do that as you're writing your copy, you will find the light bulb. You will find the light bulb. And it could be several times on a package I'm talking about where you're trying to solve a particular problem. Study creativity. Here's some books. These are the most highly recommended books I can find on creativity. I have them. I haven't read them yet. But they have very high ratings and so forth. So I'd suggest it. You checked them now. OK, so now this is Dr. Samuelson. When I was all depressed because they were killing my company, my client's company that I had worked so hard to grow, he gave me a book called Feeling Good, The New Mood Therapy. And the thing is a doorstop. And I read through it. I'll give you the executive summary. Everything I happen to use neutral. Can I think of something that really is bad that could happen? Yeah, yeah, of course. Any experience. But on a day-to-day basis in our lives, the things that happen to you are pretty much neutral. Whether we perceive them as positive or negative depends on the belief filters that we have. Now, so these belief filters have been either taught to us through religion or school or our parents. And they can be valid or they cannot be valid. So examining them from time to time is important. Let's say, for example, my client calls me up and says, you're fired. You're out of here. Now, that could be perceived as a very negative thing, especially if it was the one that's paying me on her grant a month. So that could be a very negative thing. On the other hand, there's a good chance he's holding me back. There's a very good chance. His competitors are calling me all the time trying to hire me. So maybe this is the best thing that could possibly happen to me. So now, I get a choice. I get to either view this as positive or negative. Now, my belief filters are feeding up all kinds of information to me. And they're telling me, oh, when this happens, nobody ever recovers their career. The word will get out that your client fired you. Or you deserve this because the product you sold was crap. And those thoughts create emotions. Emotions never happen just by themselves. Every emotion you've ever had was preceded by a thought. So if you're having these thoughts, the resulting emotions are debilitating. So the key is to examine your belief filters. And what's really good is, do I cover this? Yeah, I do on the next slide. You have to examine the filter through which you're seeing the event in your life. I feel like I'm screwing this up. Does any of this make sense to you? You need to examine the filter. The event was neutral. Now, you're all depressed because of the event. How come? Well, because your belief filter triggered a thought that was a negative thought. So if you go back and you look at, OK, what was the thought? Then you can determine whether it was accurate or not. And nine times out of 10, they're not. So backtrack, where did the feeling come from? What was the thought that caused it? The thought was, I'm no good. The thought was, I can't do this. The thought was, I'm not smart enough. I'm not creative enough. The thought is, when the client sees this lousy copy, he's going to fire me. And I'll be all depressed. So those are the thoughts. And they come through these belief filters that we have about ourselves. So find out what the thought was. And then ask yourself, is that valid? And what Dr. Samuelson did with me was he did this thing called emotional retrogression, and where he would take me back and help me see what the thought was. And then he would ask me, is that valid? Is it true you'll never get hired again? No. Not really. Is it true that you'll never get back to where you are today? No. Not really. And then the other thing that he had me do was he would say, what are you so worried about? And I would tell him, well, I'm worried that I'll lose my job. What would happen then? And I'd say, well, then I'd be broke. I wouldn't be able to pay the bills. What would happen then? I'd probably lose everything. Well, what would happen then? Oh, wow. I get to start all over again. Well, what would happen then? I'd probably get back to where I am today or even better. Oh, what then? And he would take me through that process and let me see that none of this was fatal. None of this stuff was as bad as I was seeing it. And it really helped me. Now, if anything I just said makes any sense to you at all, and I know I jumbled it, get the book, OK? Feeling good, the new mood therapy. And read through it, practice the exercises. I think it'll do a lot for you. OK, odds and ends. We talked about silencing our inner child and adult. Try to develop. This is just another way of saying cultivate curiosity. Pioneering mentality, what's over the next mountain? If I can get away with this and have it work, then what else could I do? How far can I push this? Whether it's an idea, whether it's a selling proposition or whatever, how far can I go with this? How can I apply this thing in different ways, this thing that's working? Booze. Let's talk about booze for a minute. Mind altering substances. This is one of the questions I got so often in Total Package. People would write in and say, can a copyrighter drink and still perform at his best level? And my answer was that I think drinking is one of the best things you can do. Yes, it kills brain cells, but only the weak ones. And but then it's called natural selection, right? So but the honest truth is that I can't drink the day before I have to write. And if I'm drinking heavily, I can't drink two days before I write. And if I've been drinking heavily for a couple of days, like when I'm on vacation, I can't write for three or four days at peak. So it does have an impact on you. Plus the lingering effect of alcohol is going to be depression, a chemically induced depression. And that's unhealthy for copywriters. So I'm not going to preach to you about the evils of alcohol here or any other mind altering substance. But just be aware that whatever you do is going to ultimately have an impact on your performance at work and manage it in a way that makes sense. Never stop thinking or suggesting, Lori hit on this? David hit on this? Carleen hit on this? I've hit on it? By constantly thinking, how can my client do this better? How can he do this more profitably? What additional value can I bring to this? You make yourself indispensable to the client because none of the other copywriters are doing that. They're just shutting up, collecting their money, and doing their job. But the minute you raise your head and say, I think it would work better if we did it this way over here, if you do it in a non-argumentative way, where you're just trying to help, you'll gain tremendous value in your client's eyes. And finally, I've been doing this 42 years. I am still hungry for more successes. Someday, as Gary said when he wrote about me one time, he says, someday Clayton will lay down his pen. I'm not sure I will ever do that until basically I'm in the ground. But the key is that you've got to stay hungry. You want that next victory. You want that next reward. I bet if we pulled the top copywriters who are here, we talked so much about money. And money is important. Money is good and all the things we've talked about. But when it comes right down to it, the most satisfying thing to me, and I bet to these other copywriters, is winning. Is winning. It validates you. It validates your self-worth. You feel like a champion when it happens. And it's so much more intense high than cashing the check. Cashing the check's great. But winning is really what it's about. So never stop. Never stop. I asked Gary one time what his main secret was for consistently writing incredible packages, direct mail packages. And he said, before he starts every promotion, he sits there and thinks and visualizes several things. First thing he visualizes is turning in the copy and having the client call him breathless over how great the copy is. The second thing he visualizes is the client calling him to tell him he's got a new control. And the third thing he visualized, and I mean by visualizing, I don't mean just mental snapshot. I mean going, what would the call be like? What would the client say? What would I say? What would I tell my wife about this? What would she say? Trying to experience this winning thing in advance. And he says, then the final thing I fantasize is going the mailbox, finding the check, and giving it to my wife. Now if you have, I mean for a guy that's validating and makes you the hero in the family, you did this for the family. So for the guy, that's a big deal. So by holding those images in his mind while he was writing copy, he felt it raised his game at every step of the way. And I think it does too. For me, I've always focused on blowing the client away. I think if I did a better job of fantasizing those other things, I'd probably be a better writer. Are there any questions or comments from anybody? You want to take a little break? Would you like to do that and come back? Or do you want to break? Or how many want to break? Come on. All right. Any questions then? Yes. Microphone. I have a question which other people may have as well that are new to this. What does it actually mean to beat a control? Like how much more sales or whatever do you need to say this beat a control? OK. Good question. And it's different in direct mail than it is online. But the general concept is that do you know what statistical reliability is? When you do a test, there's a certain amount of n or variable in that test. So the test has to be a sufficient size so that the result is reliable. That's number one. So the client will take your direct mail piece and make a mail list. He'll get a mailing list. He will do an nth name select, which means one name gets your package, the next name based on zip code will get the other guy's package, and so forth until he's built up a cell, a sufficiently large cell, 20,000 pieces being the absolute minimum in most cases. He'll then mail the two packages. He'll look at the numbers. What was your response rate? What percentage of people received your package, bought the product? Same thing for the other guy. He'll then look at the total revenue on your side versus the other side. That's important, for example, if you have a one year and a two year offer on a subscription basis, because if you have the same response rate, but he gets more two year purchases, he beats you. So they're looking for a definitive beat based on the sample size. And typically, anything under 10%, they're going to retest. Over 20%, it's convincing you win. So if you beat the other guy by 20%, you win. May I ask a second question? So this product that I'm working on, they said it's a new product. So it couldn't beat a control, or would it be beating against the control for a related product that the company has? No, no, no, no. You write a promotion for that new product that has no control. You are now the control. Yeah, whether you won or lost. The question is whether you made the money or lost the money. So here's the deal. They would basically say, if you lose the money, we have no control because they can't remail your package. It's online. It's an online. OK, so it's online. If you, in that case, you're going to be the control until somebody beats you. If nobody went up against you, they have no marketing cost. Every dollar that comes in is pure revenue. You've got the control. Simply by hiring you, they give you the control. So if I do this and they send it out, I can say I have a control? Yeah, absolutely. Yeah. Yeah, totally honest, right, Carly? Yeah. We'll face it control. Yeah. Uncontested control. I love that. Can I have that? That's like my piece. So you have the uncontested control. Yeah. OK, yes, behind you. Is it important, or is it better not to know, or does the client even reveal what the, they'll say here's our control, but do they reveal the numbers to you? I've only gotten emails saying this is the control. Yeah. I never had them tell me in advance, because there are a lot of variables. OK, so they could only give you an old number. This is what it did the last time we mailed. But what list did you mail? What's a seasonality factor? Response varies by season of the year. So that number would not really be relevant. All that matters is when they do the test, they'll come in and they'll tell you what your response rate was, what your average sale was, whether you won or lost. My next question is always, well, how much did I get beat by? I know that changing a headline, I've done it many times, can give me up to a 60% lift in response. So if I'm fairly close, 20% say from beating the control, I'll say give me one more shot. I got a headline idea. I think I can blow this out of the water. We're close enough. Another little trick to know is if you're looking at a promotion, let me make sure I get this right. If you're looking at the results on a promotion and you tied on the response rate, but the other guy won on revenues, he brought in more money, then what you need to be looking at is not the front end of the package, but the back end. You need to be looking at what's the incentive to go for the two-year offer or the multiple product offer, because you've got an average order problem at that point. So it would be much easier at that point to get your average order up than it would be to get your response up. If you pulled the same as the control, you're in good territory. You just need to work on that back end a little bit. I just wanted to add something as far as when you have the opportunity to get a chance of writing a package, you want to hedge your bet as much as possible. So even if the client's not savvy enough to ask you this, the client hires you by the package and says, okay, just give me a headline with your package. Offer more than one headline test. A headline can make a major difference in results. So I do what I call my bread and butter headline, which is the got to pay the rent with this money, so I got to make sure it's going to work. And then if you only have two chances, he says, yes, I'll give you another test panel, you can test another control, another cover or headline. You do your core, your real good one, solid bread and butter and then you go for a home run, something that's going to either just go to the moon or it's going to just die. I mean, you want to do something because it gets you a chance to break outside the box. And sometimes it's crazy. I mean, we've done some stuff and they worked or they didn't work. You don't want it to be flat. You want them to tell you this version totally bombed or it did really well because then that's what you're doing. You're pushing the envelope with that particular product. So always ask if you can give them several headlines, even if they don't test those headlines right away, write them anyway because that is the time when you are most knowledgeable about the product, you're more passionate about the product and the ideas are just coming. The hardest thing for me is I write a package and like five, six months later they come back to me and say, hey, we need more headlines. I'm like, I have no idea what this is not that gives me even a bad one. Totally shifting gears at that point. So while you're hot and heavy and excited about and focus on the product, think of as many headlines as you possibly can. Submit them and hopefully they'll mail a couple, but they don't at least have them that if your package works or comes close, they can go to another headline. A couple of things on that. First of all, I'm agreeing with Carleen, but the best way to do that, and I have some copywriters who have heard that advice. Brad Peterson's one of them. And so he'll send me a package with five different headlines. And I'm supposed to pick the one I want. It's a better deal to submit your copy as your copy. This is my headline. This is my copy. And then as the client is considering that copy to go to him and say, look, I have three other headline ideas. Would you be willing to test? And then at that point, it doesn't look like you don't know which headline you really believe in. So the bread and butter headline, he's got that. And now you say, I'd really like to have at least one more panel or two more panels to test some other ideas I have. Could I do that? If he says yes, that's great. Honey, Chris. It's kind of further along that idea. When you ask your client and they say, no, we're just going to run this one, is it better to run the one promotion first, see how it does, and then test another headline? Should you be? No, if you can test two at a time, you're better off, because you double your chances of winning. So if they say no, you just got to go with it. You got to go with your best shot then. Yeah, yeah. And just keep the other in your back pocket just in case. One other thing I want to hear what Karleen was talking about, and this is a great way to very quickly and easily double your income, right? She hit the nail on the head. The best time for you to write a package to promote a product is when you've just finished writing a package to promote that product. You've been completely immersed in that product, you know the market, you know the prospect, you know the benefits, you've got everything down. You have unused ideas, right? And so I would go to Bob King and he'd say, well, what do you want to work on next? And I'd say, well, I just did a package for health and healing. I think I'd like to do a package for health and healing. And we'll pick a completely different theme, but I'm hot to trot. I've got everything here. I have everything I need. I don't have to learn about the product. I don't have to learn about the prospect. I've got all the files. I've got the testimonials. I've edited them. I'm ready to go. So that's the time when you're going to be able to turn around a package the quickest, a new package the quickest. And I think a lot of times you'll turn out a better package than you did the first time just because of the total immersion time you've had in it. So are the thoughts, questions? Clayton, you've talked about getting familiar with your style, what you've written, and the packages many of the other writers have done in the room, and many of the writers who aren't here in the room. I'm just wondering, how do we know what's your package versus, I know once we know what's your package, we can start seeing your style. But how do we start looking for those? Yeah, it's not always easy. Paris likes to say, though, that if you can't identify a Gary Bencevenga package, when you see one, then there's something wrong. But that pre-supposes that at some point in time you knew that something was definitely a Gary package, so you could attribute certain attributes to Gary's style. I don't think there's an answer to that. I mean, I can tell you most of the stuff I write is going to be for Weiss research. There'll be other stuff out there. So you want to see my style, you want to see what I'm doing, sign up for Money in Market's newsletter, and you'll get an email or two every single day until you tell us to quit. And I click the links in the emails, and you'll go to sales pages, mini sites selling you products. So you'll get a really good taste of what I do. The boardroom, I think, is a great place to sign up. They have, do you know how many names they have? They have 48 million names on their mailing list. 48 million names. There's only 300 million people in the country. They have 48 million names on their list. So you sign up for any of their things. They have a health easing now you can sign up for. You can go ahead and buy some of their books, sign up for Bottom Line Personal Newsletter, spend a few coins. But you get on those lists, and your mailbox will start filling up with copy written by some of the best guys in the business, including these people. And you'll start getting familiar with what's working. You won't always know if it was David and Marcella or if it was Paris or all three or none of the above. But you'll be getting some really good copy to study. I think you want to see Paris's book. You should sign up for either Advanced Biotritional Score, one of the sound view. Say that again. I said if you want to see the stuff that Paris is either writing or copy-chiefing or directing us on, sign up for either buy a supplement from Advanced Biotritional. I highly recommend their probiotics. But there's a lot of others. Or sign up for their newsletters, Rowan's newsletter or Nan Fuchs, or who am I forgetting? Schallenberger.
