Yeah, the second opinion newsletter.com, realcuresletter.com, women'shealthletter.com, and then the vitamin company is advancedbionutri-tionals.com. Are you also writing for boardroom now? Yeah, I still do stuff for them. I've had the control for bottom line health and bottom line personal both for like 10 years. Wow. Yeah. 10 years. What's your royalty rate? You know, I've never gotten past the I can't charge more than Gary charges. Gary's retired and I still can't bring myself to charge more than what he would charge. So. Fair enough. There might be IRS agents in a room. We don't want them. I wanted to just say something on this note. I believe there's the who's mailing what. I believe they have a website now. It used to be in the mail. Yeah, and so if you sign up for that, they will they find out who's writing what, and they display it, and people get to comment on it and everything. So that's a good place to go. And in order to really flood yourself with samples, you should what we call seed yourself on other people's lists. Sign up, buy one product from that company, and you will start getting every other promotion for every other product that they have. And that's how you collect a file of other promotions out there. And there's a trick where you can put a fake name in it, and you can keep track of that name so that you'll see when they rent it out to someone else. So if you all of a sudden get someone else's newsletter and it has that name on it, you knew that that person rent it that other person's list. So that it's, list managers use that trick a lot. I don't know how much good it'll do you, but at least you'll know who's renting the various names and probably point out some good client potential. I want to make one correction also. On the bottom line, health, I had that sucker for a long time, and nobody could beat me. They put everybody against me. And Carleen finally beat me on half. So she's got the control for the house name. You have the house names and I have the outside. All right, yeah. So we're co-control owners on that one. Good for you. Wow, that's so cool. Thank you for that. I was going to say, if you want to see the stuff that I've written, on my website, I have a tab. It says kick butt controls. You click on it and I show, at least you'll see the covers and you'll see the client's name right there. So it's carleencole.com. And like I said, you can look and you can see some of the stuff that I have, I have, even still active now or I've had in the past. Cool. No, I was just saying, I'm listening to everything in there and having worked in acquisitions as an intern, I got to see the list panels because I was filing everybody's samples. And it got to the point where I could glance at samples and go, I think my mom wrote this. Just by getting on the verbiage and even hearing Paris' name and stuff, I'm like, I remember seeing that name on the list panels. And then because they realized, oh, you're her daughter, they'd block out the names or try to. So my mom would be like, so who's beating right now? I'm like, I'm not sure. But I think on the list, I saw your name four times. And only this one one time. You know? So, right, right. I don't work there anymore, so it's okay. But it is really interesting that if you read enough samples of different people that you can pick up on different ones' style without even realizing it. Yeah. And then you just need a name and a face to go with that particular style and you can sometimes get that from clients you talk to. This, when I think it was Schellenberger, and I'm really curious to know if this is working in financial, but for the first time, because I always, I love to click on, I get all the mailing lists and I'll just go and root around and see what's going on. And it was a video, and I was at lunch and couldn't watch the whole video. So I click it off as it normally does. And it goes down to the continue or, what is that? Continue or, yeah. The exit pop, right. This time when I said exit, and I'm pretty sure it was Schellenberger, it came up with, okay, we'll send you a free transcript. Give us your email. I've never seen that before. Yeah, brilliant. And I was at, does anybody know who that was? I think it was, maybe it wasn't Schellenberger. I tell you the company that's been doing the most messing around with exit pops, and it's very technically heavy, kind of hard to do kind of stuff, coding. Exit pops are very sensitive to change and so forth. But Lou Betancourt has been doing more of that than anybody. I know. And they've been completely changing the exit pops. We're all following the old rules where you have stay on page or exit. And he's doing these things where they're changing the options that you have on exit pops. I have probably a silly technical question. You had mentioned on your email account that you had things that went into separate file folders. I tend to get overwhelmed with the amount of email and then just start delete, delete, deleting. But I love the idea of just putting it into files so that when I wanted to look at it, it would be there, but it wouldn't take up my time. So how did you do that? Well, I don't remember. But in Outlook, you can write rules. In Outlook, you can write rules. And so you can click on a file and write a rule for that email. Whenever this comes in, put it in my this folder, that folder. So if I remember, I right clicked under Inbox and then created a new folder. And I named that folder for Motley Fool. And then I wrote a rule. And you can go to the Help section on Outlook, and it'll tell you step by step how to do it. I wrote a rule that said anything that comes from this center will go into this folder. And you could probably Google it and you find the instruction on a blog somewhere. No, the instruction's a write-in Outlook. OK, good. You can just go to the Help section of Outlook. Yeah, like that. Anybody else? Anybody else? We can always count on Chris. Just can't disappoint you on that one, Clayton. You might be talking about this tomorrow. I don't know. But when you have your traffic, like you're in a mini campaign, or like you were saying, fail fast, and you have the traffic drivers in the squeeze page, and you get the leads. And then if it does well, you may be moving on to next steps. Are you going to cover those next steps at all, or can you comment on that now? No, I can comment on it now. Basically, let's take an example. We have a report called 10 highest rated stocks under $10. And it's our most popular report right now. And so I could come to you and I could say, I need a new approach to this. We're offering this free report in return for the people to opt into money and markets aren't easy. So it's a lead producer. So I'd say we need to beat the control. And the control on lead production is based on CPL, cost per lead. So if my current control is getting me leads at the rate of $6 apiece, and you come in at $5.50, you win. So if you win, I would then come to you and I would say, OK, we're going to roll with this. My monthly budget for media for these things to drive traffic is $380,000. I can spend that much every single month without going to management. If I want to spend more than that, it's available to me, but I don't have the authority to do it without management. So I say, OK, we're going to bolster the wall. Peddle the metal. We're going to buy all of the media that we can for this and drive traffic with it. What that means is that I'm going to have this flood of leads, 40, 50, 60, 100,000 leads a month coming into my list. Now, I want to monetize those leads as fast as I can so I can recover what I spend in media costs. So I would come to you and I would say, OK, now, there are two, think about the steps. There's a traffic driver. So I'm going to say, if I spend $1 on an ad and you drive one person with that, that's good. But if you can figure out a way to drive two people with it, I've just cut my cost in half for that click through. So that's the first place we're going to start. We're going to come up with a bunch of new ideas to improve the response that we get on those traffic drivers. The second thing is the actual squeeze page. The thing with squeeze pages is people get there and abandon them without opting in. So we're going to figure out ways that we can make a squeeze page work better. Now, it could be we're going to put a different headline on it. We're still offering the same report because that's what won. But we may put a different headline on the squeeze. We may have it designed differently. We may have a add a bread crumb sign up. Before you download your report, just tell us your first name or just give us your email address. And then actually, you start with the email address. And the next question comes up, what's your first name, what's your second name? Get as much information as you can before they leave. Because now they're opted in. You've got their email address fair and square, right? So we might test that. And then the next thing, the big thing is, we know we can produce leads at a very efficient price at $5.50 per lead, which is excellent. The question is, how can we best monetize those leads? So I'm going to ask for what we call a funnel. And that would be, what happens to the lead after it comes in? We know a lot about this person now, especially important. We know that they like stocks under $10. So what's the next offer that we're going to make to them? While they're the happiest with this new relationship right at the outset, how are we going to do a little jujitsu here to get them over the line to actually become a customer? What would be the best product offer them? My answer would be heat maps, which Marcella is working on, which is 12 reports that you have access to 24 hours a day, seven days a week online of the highest-rated stocks in all of these different categories. And 10 under 10 is one of them. But all these other reports are like the 50 highest-rated tech stocks, the 50 highest-rated mining stocks, the 50 highest-rated dividend paying stocks, and so forth. So that would be the next offer, probably. The service is too expensive to offer noobs right off the bat. So we would probably offer them a bundle on a negative option basis. You know what negative option is, anybody? So negative option is when you go to a prospect and you say, you can get all 12 of these heat maps, which would normally cost you $450 a month, you can get all 12 of them for $39 for one month. And that way you can look at them, test drive them, actually invest based on these heat maps. By the end of the month, simply call this toll-free number and tell us if you don't want to continue otherwise we'll bill your credit card at the rate of $450 a month. OK? So the rationale there is 80% of the file, 80% of the people who take the offer are going to say, $450 a month, I mean, that's $5,000 a year. I really don't want to spend that much money. So I'm going to call in and cancel. So they call in and cancel. And your telemarketing department is sitting there hoping they'll call in and cancel. Because what they're going to do is they're going to downsell them. Which ones did you like? And so then you're talking to them about their own interests. And of course, that immediately goes on their record, their customer record, which heat maps are the most interested in. That tells you a lot about their investment style, right? So you try to downsell them. And we have special offers all the way down. There's also an annual offer that comes in at like $3,000 a year, which is heavily discounted off of the $5,000 they'd normally pay. And there's a lifetime offer, which comes in about $5,000. So at that point, it becomes basically an in and outbound telemarketing project. But as a copywriter, you would be responsible for working with us to structure all of those offers. So that's the answer to your question. We would test the traffic drivers, new traffic driver ideas. The squeeze page itself. And we would test a conversion funnel that would monetize these people later. So and for that, you could be very handsomely paid. I haven't worked at the actual numbers on that. But there's a reward for beating our cost per lead. And then there's another reward based on revenues, on acquisition, right? So the reward on revenues and acquisition is 10%. So I'm putting $380,000 a month out there driving leads with this thing. We could easily be making $150,000 a month easily, in which you get 15 grand every single month forever as long as we use that campaign. Plus, you're getting your commission on the lead cost on the front end on the improvement you made there. So it's a sweet deal. You do a little bit of work. You get paid to do it. And if you can give me a winner, then you stand to create the rest of this thing and collect all those other royalties. And I think the work is a lot less intense than what you would be doing writing a mag-a-log in a month. And it'd probably take a little less time. So. Again, I know that's kind of complicated. But if you sign up for Weiss, go to Weiss and. Money and Market stuff. Money and markets. And sign up. You'll start to get the emails inviting you. Just FYI, most of the products that they offer, even though they can be a little costly, there's a refund opportunity. So if you really want to see what's going on next, buy it. Put it on your credit card. You'll get refunded for it within the time period. Just make sure you do. It's usually 90 days. And you can just use that money. Keep buying other products and refunding it. And you can follow it through. So it's a really great way to learn and see exactly what Clayton's doing. But wait till the end to buy. So you see the whole series. When he says, this is your last opportunity. Don't believe him. It will be true. If I said that, it'll be true. If it's the absolute last to listen to the wording. Because sometimes it'll seem like it's the last. But what he really means is the last at this price. No, it will be your last at this price. Or the last at, you know. The other thing is you'll get a call. If you cancel, you call into cancel. And you really do cancel. Don't buy anything. You'll get a series of outbound calls from customer service attempting to convert you. So it's a whole very involved process. But it's just slick. It's hard not to buy. And even that process, knowing it as a copywriter, it's something you could tell a possible client about that they don't do. And most people are pretty bad at the customer service into things. And the ongoing sales and the upgrades. It's a really great place for you to shine. One time I was sitting around, and I just got the question in my mind. What would happen if I called Weiss Research, their inbound telemarketing group? And I called up, and I said, well, how much is a one-year subscription safe money report? And they would tell me normally $189, but they're on sale right now for $49, whatever. I said, well, how much is two years? And they would give me that price. And then I asked, how much is three years? We don't offer three years. I said, what? I said, how about why don't I take four years? I'll pay you double the two-year price. We don't do that. So man, I got on my computer and, dear Martin, your customer service department is. And it got fixed real quick. If you want to subscribe for life, you now can subscribe until the new millennium. Do you write the script, or do copywriters write the scripts that the marketing? It'll differ from company to company. And my company, our customer service guys are very strong. And they write their own scripts. If there's a particular part of the campaign I'm concerned about, I ask to see the script. And we're doing a bundle called Money Markets Ultimate. They were going to be testing in May or June. And on that, I'll review all of the scripts and probably edit them fairly heavily. Because it's a business model that's really going to direct the company going forward. So but there are companies where they don't have a customer service department. They're outsourcing it to some guy in Omaha or Utah. And in those cases, it's a good idea to get the copywriter to do the scripting. I have two quick questions. The first one I've always been too embarrassed to ask, what's the difference between a squeeze page and a landing page? OK. A squeeze page is a very short little page that just basically says, you drive traffic to it saying, get this free report, 10 highest rated stocks under $10. So they click on the link in that driver, whether it's a banner ad or an email. And they go to this little short page that basically confirms what you're about to give them. And it has certain legal language on it that gives you the right to email them any time you want to. So they then put their name and email address in these two little windows and hit Enter. And then one of two things happens. They are either auto forwarded to a download page for the special report you've offered for free. Or they're told that, and they would be told this up front, that, and I like this way better. It's not quite as slick, but you get better information. They would be told that by entering their email address here, they will receive an email within 30 seconds that will have a download link in it. You do it that way, and people are not going to give you the wrong. You know how you go to a club and an ugly guy wants to dance with you? And then he wants your phone number, right? And so you give him someone else's phone number? You make one up? Yeah. Well, so, yeah, no. She's never done that. So this is basically, don't give me your wrong email address. It's self-defeating. I can't deliver the report to you if you give me the wrong email address. So I kind of prefer that approach. And you would be sent to a landing page at that point, or a landing page? Yeah. The auto responder would come back and it would say, thank you. We're glad you're downloading this report. Here's the link. And then you would go to the landing page. Got it. In a landing page difference from a sales page? How? Typically, the terms are interchangeable. OK, you said then you would go to the landing page? No. Then you would go to a download page. And you would download your free report. Now, at that point, a couple of things can happen. You've downloaded your report. As soon as the download ends, we can forward you to a sales page if we have an upgrade. Also, the free report can have a pitch in it. So you can do both. You can do one or the other. But a lot of people, remember, what we're doing here is all about relationships. So if it feels like I'm being sold, then that's not a good thing. This is why the short squeeze page always beats long copies of squeeze pages. You don't want a barrier to entry. You want them. This needs to be an impulse move for them. So you want the short list squeeze page. Picture the report. Just enter your stuff here. We'll get it to you right away. No barrier to getting in. I don't have to read a whole bunch of stuff. I just have to give you my email address and I'm in. So then it's a relationship building business. So you've delivered this great report. They're going to love the report. It's got to be plenty of value added and all that kind of stuff. Now, you have to ask yourself, now, do I really want to press the issue right now? Or do I want to give them some other free stuff and invest in the client for a period of time, a customer for a period of time, before I ask him to spend the money? And that's where product launch formula comes in. And we'll talk about that tomorrow. But it's simply addressing every objection he can have before you offer him. Ask him to spend money. And that would be over a series of many emails over a couple of weeks, probably. Second question. A marketer contacts you and just says, hey, I want you to write a promo for this service. How important is it to know what the client's journey, prospects journey up to that point of receiving that letter? Is this something else? It is absolutely vital. Yeah. I've never thought about it until right now. And I've spoken on this many times at Bootcamp and diagrammed how it all kind of works. But it's important to understand the context that your copy is going to live in. What has happened to the prospect before he gets to your copy and what happens to him after? That way, especially if you're doing the upsell funnels, the monetization funnels. It's absolutely necessary. Because if you know what comes next, then you're going to know just how to write things at each step of the way to set that sale up. And we'll talk about this more tomorrow. But the web is changing in three very important ways. One of them is that bombastic copy, power words, is not working anymore. Just not working. It strains credulity. People are shutting down. We have to persuade with logic, which is harder than just shouting at people. There are a couple of other changes, too. A year ago, if you'd asked me what sells better long copy or short copy, I would have told you long copy every time. It's not true anymore. And I'm stealing from tomorrow. I'm not going to go there. But now there's another way to do it that's much more effective. So anyway, back to friendliness. The campaign needs to feel friendly. You make a friend first, then you make a sale. So you get them in on the lead. You spoil them around. You give them the report they asked for. Maybe you give them a couple of extra reports just for the heck of it. A welcome aboard reports. Spoil them. And then communicate with them. Give them good editorial work, good editorial articles about investing. Value-added stuff, buy this stock, sell that stock, whatever. Stuff they can actually use and act on for a period of time. Get them bonded with you. Get them to the point where they trust you. They like you. Then at that point, you can begin thinking about how to sell the next product to them. And that would be along the lines of product launch formula. Now, Paul is a student and a devotee of product launch formula, which we'll talk about tomorrow. I am not. I have talked to Jeff Walker on several occasions. I just got his videos. I understand the basic principles involved in it. And I've been stealing from it for years in little parts, little chunks. And it's made me a lot of money. But nowadays, the internet marketing world is getting tougher. And so it's really important to really understand it. That's one of the things we'll talk about tomorrow. Any other questions? In addition to what you were saying, as far as the market getting tough and the bombastic approach is not working anymore, on the health side, the FDA is really, really focusing and setting out warning letters or attempting to get marketers to cut back on a lot of the bold and crazy promises that used to work really well. So to me, it's not necessarily a bad thing. It just means the good copywriters are going to rise to the top. And the ones who are just making the hype claims or whatever are just going to be out of business. Because it really becomes more of engaging the reader, emotional writing, because we can't make a lot of the promises. An example, I had a package for Sun Clarella. I wrote five years ago. And it had passed their legal and everything else. They called me and said, hey, we have been able to beat this package. And we changed the product a little bit. And so we want to be able to, I'm sorry, it was a Lutheran from Sun Clarella, the company. So they wanted to go back with the package. They said, we want to go ahead and mail the package again. I said, well, before, I do any tweaks to the new name for you. Why don't you run it through your legal department first and see because it's been five years. Before I got to the conference, I got the package back. I'd say 60% of that package was redlined by the attorney. Now, I hate attorneys, but at least this one did do the high-risk, moderate-risk, and low-risk. So I just said, ignore the low and the moderate-risk. It's focused on the high-risk. Even then, it was still 40% of the package would have to be rewritten according to the standards of today just to be able to get it to mail. And this is a package that they mailed it for a couple of years. So things are really changing in the industry. So it really becomes more of understanding the product, understanding the prospect, and being able to just write compelling, appealing copy, addressing the needs of the people without a whole lot of hype. Johnson was famous for saying, come, let us reason together. And I think that's where we're going with copywriting these days. It's not the big emotional sale that turns everything into an impulse buy, but it's that patient persuasion, reasoning with them that's going to win out or lull. Especially in health. I mean, jeez. When I got out of health, it was already getting to be a major pain. On finance, a quick story. We had a, I was poor. I was living in Redonda Beach, California. This guy named Chris Lowe called me up and hired me to write a promo for his newsletter. And I knew enough to know that it was illegal for me to use testimonials or to refer to his track record. Back then, that was the case. And so he paid me $20,000, or agreed to pay me $20,000 to do this package. And I wrote it, and I sent in the copy, and Chris disappeared. And basically, what happened was the SEC, Securities and Exchange Commission, came in and said, no, you can't use testimonials. No, you cannot use your track record in promoting your newsletter. And so Chris was put out of business instantaneously, owing me $20,000, right? Did not make me happy. Long story short, Tom Phillips heard about this and said, this isn't going to stand. And he pulled together all the big publishers and everything and created a legal defense fund for Chris. And they took it all the way to the Supreme Court. And the Supreme Court says, you know what? This is First Amendment stuff. Chris is just expressing his opinion as an analyst. And he's not subject to regulation by the Securities Exchange Commission. That turned us into what we call a First Amendment industry. We are simply giving our opinions, and the government cannot prevent that. That happened on the financial side. And so we're protected by the Supreme Court ruling. The same thing did not happen on the health side. It hasn't gone up or it failed. It hasn't gone up to the Supreme Court or it failed. It hasn't gone up. It hasn't gone up that I know of. So what it takes is for the industry to band together and say, enough is enough. Wait for one of their big test cases where they try to nail somebody for saying that a particular substance does a particular thing. And for the industry to get together and say, OK, back off, Bubba, and put up the money to take it to the Supreme Court. And at that point, I think we'd have a fighting chance of winning on First Amendment grounds, but nobody's done that yet. How many differences with vitamins and health, you have the potential of them putting it in the power of the FDA. And that's what they're always fighting against. I mean, if it gets into one of the government agencies be running it, then you lose power. Well, it is now. That's the problem. It is now. That's the problem. So the only way to get them out is with a major court case. Right. Anything else? OK, just about this FDA, FTC stuff. The distinction has been that the FTC goes after people for false marketing claims or false advertising. And the FDA goes after you for making unapproved medical claims or health claims. Because the law says that if you're making any kind of disease claim, you're a drug. So you're allowed to sell supplements. It's legal to sell them. But as soon as you start saying arthritis or diabetes or anything, now you're a drug. Now you have to go for FDA approval. And it's got to, it's like $100 million to get FDA approval for something. So good luck with that one. But the thing has always been that you do everything. You're honest in all your advertising. And as far as the FDA stuff, different clients have different levels of comfort as to how far they want to push the envelope. There's a lot of gray areas. And it depends on the particular client's comfort level. And you ask them what they're comfortable with. And you act accordingly. But now the FDA also didn't have a lot of teeth. Because they're understaffed. They have to look at everything from food imported from China to everything else. And they just don't have the manpower. Medical devices. Say that again. Medical devices. Food, dyes, everything. So the FTC, on the other hand, they have a lot of teeth. They have a very big budget. They're very aggressive about going after people. And they have taken the position now that anything that's not FDA compliant in your copy is de facto false advertising. They've done the same. They tried to do the same thing in our industry without much success, simply because we had the low ruling. The what? The low ruling. That said that we are a First Amendment industry. So my clients, and this gets into compliance, I know everyone's really interested in compliance. And I don't think you should be. I think you should write the best copy. You know how to write the most persuasive arguments. You know how to write. Your client's compliance department will work with you to bring them into compliance. That will happen. But don't try to be an amateur attorney on this stuff. Marcella, you had one last comment. I was going to say I was going to be a little bit of a devil's advocate. And say, if you are a new aspiring writer and you're talking about writing, this is like at the highest level, trying to write a package for a supplement for healthy directions or whatever. That if you come in and you're writing a package for heart disease, I mean for a supplement that could work for the heart and you make all these claims about heart disease in it, they can't use it. There's no compliance officer on the face of this earth who could make that. You'd end up with three words left out of 36 pages. So you've got to find a middle ground. But you're going to learn as you go along. So I just don't want people to be listening to this with your eyes glazing over, freaking out, oh my God, and I got to know other rules, and I got to figure out what I can say and can't say. You will learn this as you mature in your business and your industry. And other, the in-house person, usually the marketing will coach you on what you can say. They'll let you know how extreme they're, or not extreme their positioning is. I mean, when I was at Schaefer's in-house, we had a document. It was like, here's the rules. This is where we draw the line in the sand. And before you ever wrote for us, that's what you got. So that you at least knew starting out, here's kind of where we draw our line. Because nobody wants to write a package that they call you up and say we can't use. Health resources re-approached me years after I'd left and the company had been sold. And they wanted to talk to me about writing a package for them. And I said, well, I have some questions about compliance that I need to understand how strict your compliance is. And what kinds of things, can I mention a disease name or not? Can I say diabetes, or do I have to say blood sugar balance? What are the rules? And they told me the rules. And I said, no, thanks. It was just way too strict. I didn't want to get involved with all that. But you can ask to talk to the compliance people or the attorney if you have a specific question. But again, don't get freaked out by it. Like Marcelo says, what you need to be doing is writing the best copy, you know how. And when we get to the section on your contract, you're going to see, oh my god, we've got a lawyer in the room. I'm going to be talking about contracts. He'll probably take issue with some of the stuff I say. But there are clauses that you can make sure are in your contract that protect you personally in any case. We'll talk about those tomorrow. So yes. Back to the compliance thing again. I mean, I tell my clients upfront, if I ever deliver to you a 100% compliant package, you should fire me. Because you can back off. And if I said heart disease by mistake, you can change the disease into the more compliant term. So but again, what Clay was saying, write the best package you can. Put the most passion in your package. You can't make disease claims. We know that. But you'll slip up and use the wrong words and whatnot. But write the best package. And then it's easier to back off of it and make the changes. I hate making the changes. I always have to make changes. I know. But if I have to sit here and think about the rules. In fact, the clients who give me the list of what to do, I don't even look at that thing. Because then I'll be a deer in a headlight. Because I'm going, OK, I can't say this. I'll freak out on there. I'll write the package first. And maybe I'll go back and look afterwards and see if I could tone it down before I give it to them. But most of the time, I just give it to them. I'm like, do I have the passion in here? Is this exciting you? Do you want to buy this product? The answer is yes. OK. All right, now let's make it mailable. Let's back it off from there. Yeah, it gets back to what I said earlier. When you write in copy, you cannot be going, oh, I can't say that. Oh, I can't say that. Oh, I can't say that. You've got to take it to the limit. And then let the compliance people do their job. And of course, with each package you do, you will become better at this. You'll learn more about what's permissible and what isn't. And Paris is absolutely right. Every client you work for is going to have a different bar. And we'll talk about it. In my contracts, I very explicitly say that it's the client who takes responsibility for this being in compliance, legal and regulatory compliance, prior to using the copy. And that really helps in terms of protecting me. He takes full responsibility for the copy at that point. All right? All right. All right.
